speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
कथं ज्ञानमवाप्नोति कथं मुक्तिर्भविष्यति।
वैराग्यं च कथं प्राप्तम् एतद्ब्रूहि मम प्रभो।।
transliteration: katham jnanam avapnoti katham muktir bhavishyati | vairagyam cha katham praptam etad bruhi mama prabho
hindi_meaning: हे प्रभु! ज्ञान कैसे प्राप्त होता है? मुक्ति कैसे होगी? वैराग्य कैसे प्राप्त होता है? यह मुझे बताइए।
english_meaning: How is knowledge attained? How will liberation come? How is dispassion achieved? Tell me this, O Lord.
context: King Janaka's three questions to Sage Ashtavakra that begin this extraordinary dialogue. These are the three pillars of liberation: jnana (knowledge), mukti (liberation), and vairagya (dispassion). The same questions that bring you to a practice like this.
practical_application: You are Janaka. You have everything materially — education, resources, intelligence — yet something feels incomplete. The questions themselves are the beginning of the path. The fact that you are asking them is already grace.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
मुक्तिमिच्छसि चेत्तात विषयान्विषवत्त्यज।
क्षमार्जवदयातोषसत्यं पीयूषवद्भज।।
transliteration: muktim icchasi chet tata vishayan vishavat tyaja | kshama-arjava-daya-tosha-satyam piyushavat bhaja
hindi_meaning: हे तात! यदि तू मुक्ति चाहता है, तो विषय-वस्तुओं को विष की तरह त्याग दे। और क्षमा, सरलता, दया, संतोष तथा सत्य को अमृत की तरह सेवन कर।
english_meaning: If you desire liberation, O dear one, renounce the objects of the senses as poison, and drink as nectar: forgiveness, simplicity, compassion, contentment, and truth.
context: Ashtavakra's first instruction — strikingly practical for such a radical non-dual text. Before the philosophical teaching, he gives ethical guidance: renounce vishaya (sense objects) like poison, and cultivate five virtues like nectar.
practical_application: This is the prescription: treat pornography, lust-objects, dopamine traps as vishavat — as poison. Not because they are sinful, but because they are lethal to your freedom. And cultivate the five nectars daily. This is the beginning of liberation.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
न पृथ्वी न जलं नाग्निर्न वायुर्नभ एव च।
एषां साक्षिणमात्मानं चिन्मात्रं विद्धि मुक्तये।।
transliteration: na prithvi na jalam na agnir na vayur nabha eva cha | esham sakshina-atmanam chinmatram viddhi muktaye
hindi_meaning: तू न पृथ्वी है, न जल, न अग्नि, न वायु, और न आकाश। मुक्ति के लिए स्वयं को इन सबका साक्षी, शुद्ध चेतनामात्र जान।
english_meaning: You are not earth, water, fire, air, or space. Know yourself as the witness of all these — pure consciousness alone — and thus attain liberation.
context: Ashtavakra immediately cuts to the heart: you are not any of the five elements or the body made of them. You are the witness (sakshi) of these — pure consciousness (chinmatra). This is the central teaching.
practical_application: When a craving arises — hunger, lust, anger — those are movements in the elements (fire, water, etc.) arising in your body. You, the witnessing awareness, are watching them. You are not the craving. You are the one who witnesses it.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
यदि देहं पृथक्कृत्य चिति विश्राम्य तिष्ठसि।
अधुनैव सुखी शान्तो बन्धमुक्तो भविष्यसि।।
transliteration: yadi deham prithak kritya chiti vishramya tishthasi | adhunai-va sukhi shanto bandha-mukto bhavishyasi
hindi_meaning: यदि तू शरीर को अलग करके चेतना में विश्राम करे, तो इसी क्षण सुखी, शांत और बंधनमुक्त हो जाएगा।
english_meaning: If you separate yourself from the body and rest in pure consciousness, you will become happy, peaceful, and free from bondage — right now.
context: The promise of the Ashtavakra Gita: liberation is available immediately. Not after years of practice, not in a future life. Adhunai-va — right now. If you shift your identification from body to consciousness.
practical_application: Right now, try: instead of identifying with the restless body and its urges, be the awareness that is watching the body. 'There is a body experiencing sensation. I am the awareness watching this.' Can you feel the shift? That shift is liberation.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
न त्वं वर्णाश्रमी नासि नाहं दर्पणसम्भवः।
असङ्गोऽसि निराकारो विश्वसाक्षी सुखी भव।।
transliteration: na tvam varnaashrami nasi naham darpana-sambhavah | asango si nirakaro vishva-sakshi sukhi bhava
hindi_meaning: तू न वर्ण का है, न आश्रम का, तू आँखों से दिखने वाला नहीं है। तू असंग (निर्लिप्त), निराकार, विश्व का साक्षी है — सुखी रह।
english_meaning: You belong to no caste or stage of life. You are not what the eyes perceive. You are unattached, formless, the witness of the world. Be happy.
context: Ashtavakra dismantles identity labels: you are not defined by caste (brahmin/kshatriya), stage of life (student/householder), or what others see. You are the formless witness — independent of all social constructs.
practical_application: You are not 'a student who fails.' You are not 'an addict.' You are not 'a sinner.' You are the formless witnessing consciousness in which these stories play. Stop identifying with your story. Be the screen, not the movie.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
ज्ञानवान्दुर्मतिर्नाहं देहोऽहं स्वच्छचेतनः।
हेयोपादेयनिर्मुक्तः स्थितोऽहं मुक्त एव हि।।
transliteration: jnana-van dur-matir na aham deho ham svaccha-chetanah | heya-upadeyanirnukto sthito ham mukta eva hi
hindi_meaning: मैं ज्ञानवान हूँ, दुर्मति नहीं। मैं शरीर नहीं, शुद्ध चेतना हूँ। मैं त्याग-ग्रहण से मुक्त हूँ — मैं वास्तव में मुक्त ही हूँ।
english_meaning: I am not this dull-minded person; I am not the body. I am pure consciousness. Free from what is to be rejected or accepted — I am in truth already liberated.
context: Ashtavakra speaks in the first person to model the state of self-recognition. The liberated person's internal declaration: I am not the confused, wanting mind. I am pure consciousness, already free.
practical_application: Affirmation practice rooted in Ashtavakra: 'I am not the craving. I am not the confusion. I am pure consciousness — already free. I am muktah.' Say this. Mean it. The recognition itself is liberation.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
एकमेव सदामृत्यं विश्वमेव हि बाधितम्।
नाहं देहो न मे देहो जीवो नाहमहं हि चित्।।
transliteration: ekam eva sada-mrityam vishvam eva hi badhitam | naham deho na me deho jivo naham aham hi chit
hindi_meaning: वह एक ही सदा अमृत है, सारा जगत बाधित (मिथ्या प्रतीत होने वाला) है। मैं न शरीर हूँ, न शरीर मेरा है — न जीव हूँ। मैं तो चित् (चेतना) हूँ।
english_meaning: The One alone is eternal — the immortal. The entire world appears and is contradicted. I am not the body, nor does the body belong to me. I am not the individual soul. I am Consciousness.
context: Ashtavakra's ontological declaration: among all that appears and disappears, only the One — pure Consciousness — is truly real and eternal. Everything else, including the 'jiva' (individual soul), is a superimposition.
practical_application: The urge, the habit, the 'person who keeps failing' — these are all within the dream of the jiva. But the awareness watching all this — that is aham chit, the real I. Rest in that.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
मुक्ताभिमानी मुक्तो हि बद्धो बद्धाभिमान्यपि।
किंवदन्तीह सत्येयं या मतिः सा गतिर्भवेत्।।
transliteration: mukta-abhimani mukto hi baddho baddha-abhimany api | kim-vadanti iha satyeyam ya matih sa gatir bhavet
hindi_meaning: जो मुक्त मानता है वह मुक्त है, जो बद्ध मानता है वह बद्ध है। यहाँ यही प्रसिद्ध सत्य है — जैसी बुद्धि होती है वैसी ही गति होती है।
english_meaning: One who thinks of himself as free is free indeed; one who thinks of himself as bound remains bound. Here is the well-known saying: as you think, so you become.
context: Perhaps the most practically important verse of the Ashtavakra Gita. Liberation is not a future achievement — it is a present self-identification. The mind that says 'I am bound' creates bondage; the mind that says 'I am free' creates freedom.
practical_application: Stop saying 'I am an addict.' Stop saying 'I always fail.' These are identity statements, and they create reality. Start saying: 'I am free. I am in the process of revealing my freedom.' Mukta-abhimani muktah — the one who thinks himself free IS free.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
अहो निरञ्जनः शान्तो बोधोऽहं प्रकृतेः परः।
एतावन्तमहं कालं मोहेनैव विडम्बितः।।
transliteration: aho niranjana shanto bodho ham prakriteh parah | etavantam aham kalam mohenaiva vidambitah
hindi_meaning: आश्चर्य! मैं निरंजन (निर्मल), शांत, चेतनास्वरूप हूँ — प्रकृति से परे। इतने समय तक मैं मोह से ही छला जाता रहा।
english_meaning: Ah! I am spotless, peaceful, pure awareness — beyond nature. For so long I have been deceived by illusion!
context: Janaka's first response after Ashtavakra's teaching — the moment of recognition. 'Aho!' — the Sanskrit exclamation of delightful surprise. This is the taste of liberation: sudden clarity that one has always been free.
practical_application: There will come a moment — maybe in meditation, maybe after a long walk, maybe at 3 AM — when you suddenly feel: 'I am not this craving body. I am something else entirely. Something vast and still.' When that happens, that is your Janaka moment.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
यथा प्रकाशयाम्येको देहमेनं तथा जगत्।
अतो मम जगत्सर्वं अथवा न च किञ्चन।।
transliteration: yatha prakashayamy eko deham enam tatha jagat | ato mama jagat sarvam athava na cha kinchana
hindi_meaning: जैसे मैं अकेला इस शरीर को प्रकाशित करता हूँ, उसी प्रकार सारे जगत को। इसलिए या तो यह सारा जगत मेरा है, या फिर कुछ भी नहीं।
english_meaning: Just as I alone illuminate this body, so too I illuminate the whole world. Therefore either the entire world is mine — or nothing is.
context: Janaka's recognition expands: if I am the consciousness that illuminates my body, I must be the consciousness that illuminates all bodies — all of existence. The one light shines through all.
practical_application: This is the shift from ego-consciousness to pure consciousness. You are not a separate entity in a world of other separate entities. You are the awareness in which 'entities' appear. This changes everything about how you relate to others.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
शरीरं स्वर्गनरकौ बन्धमोक्षौ भयं तथा।
कल्पनामात्रमेवैतत् किं मे कार्यं चिदात्मनः।।
transliteration: shariram svarga-narakau bandha-mokshau bhayam tatha | kalpana-matram evai-tat kim me karyam chid-atmanah
hindi_meaning: शरीर, स्वर्ग-नरक, बंधन-मोक्ष, भय — ये सब केवल कल्पना मात्र हैं। मुझ चिदात्मा को इनसे क्या प्रयोजन?
english_meaning: Body, heaven and hell, bondage and liberation, fear — all these are mere imagination. What do I, who am pure consciousness, have to do with these?
context: Janaka goes further: even the concept of liberation (moksha) is, from the perspective of pure consciousness, a concept. The ocean doesn't need to seek water. You don't need to seek freedom — you ARE freedom.
practical_application: The deepest teaching: stop fearing hell and seeking heaven. Stop fearing bondage and seeking liberation. Those are all concepts appearing in consciousness. Just BE the consciousness. This is the most advanced stage — resting in what you already are.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
देहाभिमानपाशेन चिरं बद्धोऽस्मि भावना।
बोधोऽहमिति यज्ज्ञानं तेन मुक्तोऽस्म्यहं चिरात्।।
transliteration: deha-abhimana-pashena chiram baddho smi bhavana | bodho ham iti yaj-jnanam tena mukto smy aham chirat
hindi_meaning: देहाभिमान की रस्सी से मैं बहुत समय से बंधा था। 'मैं बोध (ज्ञान-चेतना) हूँ' — इस ज्ञान से मैं बहुत समय के बाद मुक्त हुआ।
english_meaning: I have been bound for a long time by the rope of body-identification. By the knowledge 'I am pure awareness' — by this alone I am freed after so long.
context: Janaka identifies the one cause of bondage: deha-abhimana — identification with the body. And the one cure: the knowledge 'bodho ham' — I am awareness. The rope of bondage is psychological, not physical.
practical_application: The addiction is maintained by deha-abhimana — believing you are the body with its hungers. The cure is this shift: bodho ham — I am awareness. The one who watches the craving cannot be the craving itself.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
अहो भुवनकल्लोलैर्विचित्रैर्द्रष्टुमात्मनि।
भ्रमयन्ति तथाप्येते स्वस्थं मां न स्पृशन्ति हि।।
transliteration: aho bhuvana-kallolai vichit-rair drashtum atmani | bhramayanti tathapi ete svastha-m mam na sprishanti hi
hindi_meaning: आश्चर्य! ये विचित्र-विचित्र संसार की लहरें मुझ आत्मा में दिखाई देती हैं। ये चाहे भले घुमाएँ, लेकिन स्वस्थ (अपने में स्थित) मुझे छू नहीं सकतीं।
english_meaning: How wonderful! In me, who am pure consciousness, the waves of the universe with all their variety arise and play — yet they cannot touch me who am established in my own nature.
context: Janaka's joy-filled recognition that the world — including all its fluctuations, temptations, dramas — arises within consciousness but cannot touch consciousness itself. Like waves in an ocean — the waves cannot wet the ocean.
practical_application: Urges, thoughts, emotions — they are all waves arising in the ocean of your consciousness. The ocean can contain any wave without being disturbed. Practice being the ocean, not the wave.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
विहाय वैरिणं काममर्थं चानर्थसंकुलम्।
धर्ममप्येतयोर्हेतुं सर्वत्रानादरं कुरु।।
transliteration: vihaya vairina kamam artham cha anartha-sankulam | dharmam api etayor hetum sarvatra anadarad kuru
hindi_meaning: शत्रुस्वरूप काम को, अनर्थ से भरे अर्थ को और इन दोनों के साधन धर्म को भी छोड़कर सब चीजों में अनादर का भाव रखो।
english_meaning: Renounce desire, which is the enemy; wealth, which is full of calamity; and even dharma, which is a means to these two. Be indifferent to everything.
context: Ashtavakra is speaking to the most advanced student. Even dharma — when pursued as a means to acquire kama (pleasure) and artha (wealth) — is a subtle bondage. True freedom is beyond even the pursuit of 'good' outcomes.
practical_application: This is the advanced teaching: even 'virtuous' actions done with ego-motivation are binding. The goal is action from pure being, not from strategy. This is the highest yoga — action without any agenda, not even liberation.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
मोक्षो विषयवैरस्यं बन्धो वैषयिको रसः।
एतावदेव विज्ञानं यथेच्छसि तथा कुरु।।
transliteration: moksho vishaya-vairasyam bandho vaishayiko rasah | etavad eva vijnanam yatha icchasi tatha kuru
hindi_meaning: विषयों से वैराग्य ही मोक्ष है। विषयों में रस (रुचि/चाहत) ही बंधन है। बस इतना ही ज्ञान है — अब जैसी इच्छा हो वैसा करो।
english_meaning: Dispassion toward sense objects is liberation; attachment to sense objects is bondage. This alone is the knowledge. Do now as you please.
context: Ashtavakra compresses the entire teaching into one sutra and then, daringly, says 'do as you please' — because if you have truly understood, your pleasure will automatically align with liberation.
practical_application: Vairagya (dispassion) is liberation. Rasa (craving) is bondage. The formula is exact. You know exactly what binds you and what frees you. Now — as Ashtavakra says — act accordingly. No more excuses.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
आश्चर्यं! सुधीभिः पुरुषैरवैदिकं
ज्ञानाद् भयं तव वित्तत्त्वात् किम्।
यदा यदा स्वयं द्रष्टृदृश्यौ
तदा स्वयं शेतेऽस्ति नेत्रमात्रे।।
transliteration: ascharyam! sudhir atmavit param brahmaiva bhavati
hindi_meaning: यह आश्चर्य है कि आत्मज्ञानी परब्रह्म ही हो जाता है।
english_meaning: How wondrous! The wise person who knows the Self becomes Brahman itself.
context: The astonishing declaration of the Ashtavakra Gita: self-knowledge does not lead to Brahman — it IS Brahman knowing itself. The knower and the known collapse.
practical_application: Every moment of honest self-observation — 'there is an urge appearing in me' — is the Self knowing itself. These small moments of awareness are infinitely more valuable than hours of performance without awareness.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
तदा बन्धो यदा चित्तं किञ्चिद् वाञ्छति शोचति।
किञ्चिन् मुञ्चति गृह्णाति किञ्चिद् कुप्यति तुष्यति।।
transliteration: tada bandho yada chittam kinchid vanchati shochati | kinchit munchati grihnati kinchid kupyati tushyati
hindi_meaning: तब बंधन है जब मन कुछ चाहता है, कुछ के लिए शोक करता है, कुछ छोड़ता है, कुछ ग्रहण करता है, कुछ पर क्रोध करता है और कुछ से प्रसन्न होता है।
english_meaning: Bondage is when the mind desires something, grieves over something, rejects something, accepts something, is angry at something, or delights in something.
context: Ashtavakra's definition of bondage is purely psychological: any movement of want/rejection/anger/delight in the mind is bondage. Liberation is not the absence of experience but the absence of mental grasping at experience.
practical_application: Notice: the mind craving, grieving, rejecting, accepting, getting angry or delighted — THAT is bondage. Not the external event. The antidote is not to stop experiences but to stop the mental reaction of grasping and pushing away.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
तदा मुक्तिर्यदा चित्तं न वाञ्छति न शोचति।
न मुञ्चति न गृह्णाति न कुप्यति न तुष्यति।।
transliteration: tada muktir yada chittam na vanchati na shochati | na munchati na grihnati na kupyati na tushyati
hindi_meaning: तब मुक्ति है जब मन न कुछ चाहता है, न शोक करता है, न छोड़ता है, न ग्रहण करता है, न क्रोध करता है और न प्रसन्न होता है।
english_meaning: Liberation is when the mind does not desire, does not grieve, does not reject, does not accept, is not angry, and does not delight.
context: The precise definition of liberation (mukti) — it is the absence of the six movements of grasping described in the previous verse. This is not emotional numbness but a profound equanimity that underlies all experience.
practical_application: This is the goal: not suppression of experience, but non-grasping. The person who can feel an urge fully and not be moved to act on it — who can feel it without fighting it, without wanting it, without being angry at it — that person is free.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
बन्धो मोक्षश्च तौ नास्तः सत्त्वे मुक्ते स्वभावतः।
आत्मन्येव जगत्सर्वं विचारेण विभाव्यते।।
transliteration: bandho mokshash cha tau nastah sattve mukte svabhavato | atmany eva jagat sarvam vicharen vibhavyate
hindi_meaning: बंधन और मोक्ष दोनों नहीं हैं जब सत्त्व (चित्त की शुद्धता) स्वभाव से ही मुक्त हो। विचार (तत्त्वज्ञान) से सारा जगत आत्मा में ही देखा जाता है।
english_meaning: For the one whose pure being is naturally liberated, bondage and liberation both cease to exist. Through inquiry, the entire universe is seen as residing in the Self.
context: The most radical statement: the truly liberated person transcends even the distinction between bondage and liberation. These are concepts in duality. Pure being is beyond both.
practical_application: You may not be at this stage yet. But the direction is clear: move from 'I am bound and seeking liberation' toward 'I am the awareness in which both bondage and liberation appear as concepts.' The inquiry itself is the path.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
भव विगतखेदः स्वस्थचित्तः प्रशान्तः
हृदयमधिवसन् ब्रह्म किञ्चिच्चिदात्मा।
सुखमतिनिभृतं प्राप्नुयाद् योगयुक्तस्
तदतिमधुरमेतन्नान्यदस्तीति विद्धि।।
transliteration: bhava vigata-khedah svastha-chittah prashantah | hridayam adhivasan brahma kinchin chid-atma | sukham ati-nibhritam prapnuyad yoga-yuktas | tad ati-madhuram etan nanyad astiti viddhi
hindi_meaning: खेद (दुःख) से रहित, स्वस्थ-चित्त, प्रशांत होकर, हृदय में स्थित ब्रह्म-चेतनास्वरूप में विश्राम करो। योगयुक्त पुरुष अत्यंत निभृत (गहन) सुख पाता है। वह सुख अत्यंत मधुर है — जान लो कि उसके अतिरिक्त कुछ नहीं है।
english_meaning: Be free of sorrow. Be composed in mind. Be peaceful. Resting in the heart as the Brahman-consciousness. The one absorbed in yoga attains a deep and hidden happiness. Know that this happiness is supremely sweet — there is nothing else.
context: Ashtavakra describes the quality of the joy that comes from self-abidance — not excitement or pleasure, but a deep, quiet happiness (ati-nibhritam sukham). This is the ananda of the Upanishads — not a sensation but a fundamental quality of being.
practical_application: The happiness you seek in sexual pleasure is a pale shadow of this deeper happiness. Brahmananda — the joy of self-abidance — is described as 'supremely sweet.' This is what brahmacharya is building toward: access to this deeper reservoir of joy.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
यस्य बोधोदये तावत् स्वप्नवद् भवति भ्रमः।
ताव् मयापि जागृति स्वप्न एव यथा तथा।।
transliteration: yasya bodhodaye tavat svapnavad bhavati bhramah | tav mayapi jagriti svapna eva yatha tatha
hindi_meaning: जिसमें ज्ञान का उदय हुआ हो, उसके लिए यह जगत-भ्रम स्वप्न जैसा हो जाता है। उसी प्रकार जागृत में भी स्वप्न ही है।
english_meaning: For the one in whom knowledge has dawned, the confusion of the world becomes like a dream. Even the waking state becomes like a dream.
context: The classic teaching of Advaita: once Brahman-knowledge arises, the world is not destroyed but is seen for what it is — a vivid dream arising in consciousness. The dreamer is not confused about dream objects once they wake.
practical_application: The urges and cravings that seem so solid and real — when seen with the light of awareness, they become like dream-objects. Not unreal exactly, but not the solid, compelling things they seemed to be. This is vairagya from knowledge, not from force.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
स्वभावाद् यस्य नैवास्ति संसारस्य भयं कण।
कस्य वैराग्यमुत्पन्नं कः मुमुक्षुः कुतो जगत्।।
transliteration: svabhavad yasya naivasti samsarasya bhayam kana | kasya vairagyam utpannam kah mumukshuh kuto jagat
hindi_meaning: जिसे स्वभाव से ही संसार का लेशमात्र भय नहीं, उसे वैराग्य कैसा? मुमुक्षा कैसी? जगत कहाँ?
english_meaning: For one who by nature has not even a particle of fear of the world — what need of dispassion? What of liberation? What world?
context: The highest state: when even the concepts of vairagya (renunciation) and mumukshu (seeker of liberation) dissolve — because the one who is naturally established in their true nature has nothing to renounce and nowhere to go.
practical_application: You are not yet at this stage, and that is fine. The path is: practice vairagya (renunciation) → gain jnana (knowledge) → discover that the one who is fully established in knowledge has no need for renunciation. Practice is the boat; wisdom is the shore.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
आचक्ष्व शृणु वा तात नानाशास्त्राण्यनेकशः।
आत्मविस्मृतिरेवैका नरकायेति निश्चिनु।।
transliteration: achakshva shriunu va tata nana-shastrani anekashaha | atma-vismritir evaika narakaye iti nishchinu
hindi_meaning: हे तात! चाहे हजारों शास्त्र पढ़ो, सुनो — परंतु निश्चित जानो कि आत्मा की विस्मृति ही एकमात्र नरक है।
english_meaning: O dear one, you may read or hear thousands of scriptures — but know for certain that forgetfulness of the Self alone is hell.
context: A powerful redefinition of hell: not a place of punishment but the state of forgetting one's true nature as pure consciousness. Conversely, heaven is simply remembering who you are.
practical_application: Hell is not some future punishment. Hell is the state of running from urge to urge, dopamine hit to dopamine hit, completely identified with the body-mind, completely forgetting that you are free. Sound familiar? That is the only hell there is.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
तत्त्वविज्ञानसन्दंशमादाय हृदयोदरात्।
नाना मोहशलाकाभिर्निष्कृष्य मलमात्मनः।।
transliteration: tattva-vijnana-sandamsham adaya hridayo-darat | nana moha-shalakabhir nishkrishya malam atmanah
hindi_meaning: आत्म-ज्ञान की चिमटी से हृदय के भीतर से अनेक मोह की सलाखों को निकालकर आत्मा का मल दूर करो।
english_meaning: With the tongs of self-knowledge, drawing out the many rods of delusion from the cavity of the heart, remove the dross of the Self.
context: A vivid metaphor: use the instrument of self-knowledge (tattva-vijnana) as tongs to extract the thorns of delusion from the heart. This is an active, surgical process.
practical_application: Every time you observe a craving with clear witnessing awareness — 'this is an urge, not me' — you are applying the tongs of self-knowledge. Each observation is a thorn removed. Do it daily.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
अहो चित्तं चित्ततया ज्ञातं
जानन्नज्ञानं कथमात्मनः स्यात्।
आकाशवन्निर्मलतारमेयं
स्फुरत्यहो विश्वमिदं मयीव।।
transliteration: aho chittam chittataya jnatam | janann ajnanam katham atmanah syat | akashavan nirmalatara-meyam | sphurati aho vishvam idam mayi-va
hindi_meaning: आश्चर्य! मन को मन की तरह जाना। जानते हुए आत्मा का अज्ञान कैसे हो सकता है? आकाश जैसा निर्मल, अत्यंत स्वच्छ — आश्चर्य! यह सारा जगत मुझमें ही प्रकाशित होता है।
english_meaning: How wondrous! I have known the mind as mind. Knowing this, how can ignorance of the Self persist? Pure as the sky, immeasurably clear — how wondrous that this entire universe shines within me!
context: Janaka's ecstatic recognition in Chapter 18 — the longest chapter, Janaka's deepest realization. The universe is not outside him — it shines within him, as his own consciousness.
practical_application: This is the destination: to recognize that the world — including all its beauty and all its temptations — shines within your own consciousness, not outside it. You are the space in which experience arises. Nothing can bind the space.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
कल्लोलैर्भिद्यमानस्य सिन्धोरुद्भूतशब्दवत्।
सर्वस्यैव चिदानन्देऽस्तित्वं प्रकटमादरात्।।
transliteration: kallolai bhidyamanasya sindhor udbhuta-shabdavat | sarvasya-eva chid-anande astitvam prakatam adarat
hindi_meaning: जैसे लहरों से टकराते समुद्र का शब्द उत्पन्न होता है, उसी प्रकार सब की सत्ता चिदानंद में स्पष्ट रूप से प्रकट होती है।
english_meaning: As the sound arises from the ocean being struck by waves, so the existence of everything is clearly revealed in Consciousness-Bliss (chidananda).
context: The beautiful ocean metaphor: individual existences (waves) are nothing but the ocean (consciousness-bliss) in motion. The sound they make — the variety of the world — is consciousness expressing itself.
practical_application: You are the ocean. Your urges are waves. They are not foreign enemies attacking you from outside. They are your own consciousness taking a temporary shape. The ocean is not afraid of its own waves.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
न सुखं न दुःखं न कालो न देशो
न जन्म न मृत्युश्चिताहं शिवोऽहम्।
गतकल्मषाय प्रशान्ताय तस्मात्
नमः स्वस्वरूपाय शान्ताय साक्षिणे।।
transliteration: na sukham na duhkham na kalo na desho | na janma na mrityush chit-aham shivo-ham | gata-kalmashaya prashantaya tasmat | namah sva-svarupaya shantaya sakshine
hindi_meaning: न सुख, न दुःख, न काल, न देश, न जन्म, न मृत्यु — मैं चेतना हूँ, शिव हूँ। पापरहित, प्रशांत — उस अपने स्वरूप को, शांत साक्षी को नमस्कार।
english_meaning: No pleasure, no pain, no time, no space, no birth, no death — I am consciousness, I am Shiva. Free of blemish, peaceful — salutations to my own nature, the peaceful witness.
context: Janaka's final declaration — beyond all pairs of opposites. Not even pleasure is mine, nor pain. I am the consciousness in which these appear. Chit-aham, Shivo-ham — I am consciousness, I am Shiva.
practical_application: Chit-aham — I am consciousness. Shivo-ham — I am Shiva, the auspicious one, the pure one. These are your true names. When the practice is hard, whisper these to yourself. Not as delusion but as the deepest truth.

speaker: Janaka
sanskrit:
यस्य बोधोऽस्ति चित्तेऽस्मिन् सोऽपि बद्धो न मुच्यते।
यस्य नास्ति चिदात्मैव स एव मुक्त उच्यते।।
transliteration: yasya bodho sti chitte smin sopi baddho na muchyate | yasya nasti chid-atmaiva sa eva mukta uchyate
hindi_meaning: जिसके चित्त में भी बोध (ज्ञान) का अहंकार है, वह बद्ध है और मुक्त नहीं होता। जिसके (चित्त में) चेतनास्वरूप भी नहीं है — वही मुक्त कहलाता है।
english_meaning: One who still has the notion of 'I have knowledge' in the mind is bound and not liberated. Only the one who has no such notion — who simply is pure consciousness, without claim — that person alone is called liberated.
context: The subtlest teaching: even the thought 'I am liberated' or 'I have knowledge' is a subtle form of ego and bondage. True liberation is so natural that there is no one left to claim it.
practical_application: Don't be attached to your practice. Don't say 'I am on day 47 and becoming enlightened.' Just do the practice, honestly, quietly, without making a story about it. The one who is truly building is not making a big deal about building.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
अकिञ्चनस्य दान्तस्य शान्तस्य समचेतसः।
न कश्चिद्विद्यते शत्रुर्न च बन्धुः कदाचन।।
transliteration: akincha-nasya dantasya shantasya sama-chetasah | na kashchid vidyate shatrur na cha bandhuh kadachana
hindi_meaning: जो अकिञ्चन (अपरिग्रही), दान्त (इन्द्रियजित), शांत और समभाव वाला है — उसका न कोई शत्रु है, न कोई मित्र।
english_meaning: For one who is free of possessiveness, whose senses are controlled, who is peaceful and even-minded — there is no enemy, and there is no friend.
context: The state of the jivanmukta (living liberated one): because there is no ego-claim to protect, there is no enemy. Because there is no attachment, there is no 'friend' in the grasping sense. Only universal compassion remains.
practical_application: As brahmacharya builds equanimity, notice this: people who previously 'triggered' you trigger you less. Not because you suppressed the reaction but because the possessive, grasping ego has weakened. This is a real psychological shift.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
लयविनाशनौ युक्त्वा न भवेतामिमौ यदि।
तदा द्वाभ्यामपि मुक्तस्त्वं चिदानन्दो भविष्यसि।।
transliteration: laya-vinashanau yuktvaa na bhavetam imau yadi | tada dvabhyam api muktas tvam chidanando bhavishyasi
hindi_meaning: यदि ये दो — लय (समाधि में लीन होना) और विनाश (विक्षेप/विचलन) — न हों, तो तू इन दोनों से मुक्त होकर चिदानंद हो जाएगा।
english_meaning: If these two — laya (absorption, laziness) and vikkshepa (distraction, agitation) — can be avoided, then freed from both, you will become pure consciousness-bliss.
context: Ashtavakra identifies the two enemies of meditation: laya (drowsiness, mental dissolution) and vikshepa (distraction, mental agitation). Too much of either keeps one from the alert, clear consciousness of liberation.
practical_application: In your meditation, you face both: sometimes the mind goes dull and sleepy (laya), sometimes restless and distracted (vikshepa). The practice is to navigate between them — alert without agitation, still without drowsiness. This is the razor's edge.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
साक्षी विशुद्धो बोधात्मा प्रत्यग्व्यापी निरंजनः।
बाह्यासक्तिमता दुःखं न बाह्यासक्तिमृच्छति।।
transliteration: sakshi vishuddho bodhatma pratyag-vyapi niranjana | bahya-asaktimata duhkham na bahya-asaktim ricchhati
hindi_meaning: साक्षी, विशुद्ध, बोधात्मा, अंतर्व्यापी, निरंजन (निर्मल) — बाह्य आसक्ति से दुःख होता है। बाह्य आसक्ति न हो तो दुःख नहीं।
english_meaning: The witness, pure, the intelligence-Self, pervading all from within, unstained — suffering comes from attachment to external objects. Without attachment to the external, there is no suffering.
context: A practical analysis of suffering: it is attachment to the external (bahya-asakti) that creates duhkha. The witness — pure, all-pervading, unstained — is already free. When you rest in the witness, external attachment weakens.
practical_application: Your suffering around urges is bahya-asakti — attachment to external sense-objects. The cure is not suppression but turning inward to rest in the witness. Try in the moment of urge: 'I am the witness. This is an object arising in consciousness.' Feel the shift.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
न त्वं देहो न ते देहो भोक्ता कर्ता न वा भवान्।
चिद्रूपोऽसि सदा साक्षी निरपेक्षः सुखं चर।।
transliteration: na tvam deho na te deho bhokta karta na va bhavan | chid-rupo si sada sakshi nirapekshah sukham chara
hindi_meaning: तू न शरीर है, न तेरा शरीर है। तू न भोक्ता है, न कर्ता। तू सदा चेतनास्वरूप साक्षी है — निरपेक्ष, सुख से विचर।
english_meaning: You are not the body, nor does the body belong to you. You are neither the enjoyer nor the doer. You are always the witness of the nature of consciousness — independent, move about happily.
context: The complete teaching in one verse: not the body, not its owner, not the experiencer, not the actor. Always the witnessing consciousness. And from this recognition — nirapekshah (independent) and sukham chara (move about happily).
practical_application: You are not the person who watches pornography. You are not the addict. You are the witness of consciousness — pure, independent. The body and mind have habits; you are not the body and mind. Sukham chara — move about happily.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
कुत्र भ्रमो ममाज्ञस्य तत्त्वज्ञस्य कुतो भ्रमः।
कुतः संसारबाधा वा स्थितस्य स्वात्मनि प्रभोः।।
transliteration: kutra bhramo mama-ajnasya tattva-jnasya kuto bhramah | kutah samsara-badha va sthitasya sva-atmani prabhoh
hindi_meaning: अज्ञानी को कहाँ भ्रम नहीं? और तत्त्वज्ञ को भ्रम कहाँ? अपनी आत्मा में स्थित प्रभु को संसार की बाधा कहाँ?
english_meaning: Where is there no delusion for the ignorant? And where is there delusion for the knower of truth? Where is there any harassment from the world for one established in the Self?
context: A pair of rhetorical questions: the ignorant are always confused because they identify with the body-mind. The one who knows their nature as consciousness is never confused. The one established in the Self is untouched by worldly problems.
practical_application: The goal of the practice is not to suppress urges by force — it is to establish yourself in your true nature. From that establishment, the 'harassment' of urges naturally diminishes. Establishment is the cure.

speaker: Ashtavakra
sanskrit:
स्वभावाद् विरतस्यैव स्वभावो जायते परम्।
स्वभावात् परमात्मानं जानन् मुक्तो विचरति।।
transliteration: svabhavad viratasya-eva svabhavo jayate param | svabhavat paramatmanam janan mukto vicharati
hindi_meaning: जो स्वभाव (प्रकृति के आवेगों) से विरत हो जाता है, उसका परम स्वभाव जागृत होता है। परमात्मा को स्वभावतः जानकर वह मुक्त होकर विचरण करता है।
english_meaning: For one who has turned away from (the compulsions of) one's natural tendencies, the supreme nature arises. Knowing the Supreme Self by nature, one moves about freely — liberated.
context: The promise of the Ashtavakra Gita's final chapter: when you turn away from the compulsive tendencies of body-mind (svabhava), a higher nature (param svabhava) arises naturally. This higher nature knows the Supreme Self directly.
practical_application: Every day you turn away from the compulsive tendency — even once, even briefly — you allow the param svabhava (higher nature) to breathe. This higher nature accumulates with each choice. Over 60-90 days, it becomes the dominant force.
